event: Karen is Drew's world. She is warm, present, and anxious in equal measure. Greg works late at Alcatel — home by 7 most nights, later during product cycles. Drew's first word is 'mama.' His second is 'no.' He cries when Karen leaves the room and stops when she comes back. The loop is reliable. The loop is everything.
developmental_impact: Primary attachment forming — secure base established, but Karen's vigilance transmits as ambient alertness

event: Oklahoma City bombing. Drew is two — he doesn't know what happened, but he knows the TV was on all day and Karen held him tighter than usual for a week. Greg drove to Oklahoma with his company's disaster relief team. He was gone four days. Drew asked for him every morning.
developmental_impact: First experience of father's absence during ambient threat. Karen's anxiety spikes. Drew absorbs: danger is real but abstract, and Dad leaves when it comes.

event: Preschool at First United Methodist. Drop-off is brutal for the first two weeks — Drew clings to Karen's leg, face pressed into her knee. She peels him off gently, cries in the car. By week three he walks in on his own, but checks the door twice. He learns to build with blocks, share crayons, wait his turn. Megan starts first grade and comes home with gold stars. The refrigerator isn't big enough for all of them.
developmental_impact: Separation anxiety resolves to manageable watchfulness. First exposure to social comparison through sibling achievement.

event: Two Plano teenagers die of heroin overdoses. Then three more. The story is on every local news broadcast. Karen and Greg talk about it after the kids are in bed, but Drew hears the tone — low, scared, angry — through the heating vent in his room. Karen starts asking more questions about who Megan plays with. The parenting culture in Willowcreek shifts toward surveillance. Playdates require more vetting. The world outside the house becomes a place that needs managing.
developmental_impact: Ambient community trauma absorbed without comprehension. The message imprints: the neighborhood looks safe but isn't. Vigilance is love.

event: Kindergarten at Mendenhall Elementary. Drew is quiet, compliant, good with puzzles. His teacher writes on his report card: 'Drew is a pleasure to have in class. He could participate more in group discussions.' He makes a friend — Connor Huang, who lives on the next street over. Connor talks enough for both of them. They build Lego spaceships on Connor's bedroom floor every Saturday while their moms drink coffee downstairs.
developmental_impact: Attachment style consolidating — secure enough for friendship, but sociability remains low. Connor provides a social bridge. The teacher's comment establishes a pattern: adults like Drew's quietness but want more from him.

event: Columbine. April 20. Drew is in kindergarten. The teachers don't tell them what happened but they can tell something is wrong — the way Mrs. Kim kept looking at the door, the way pickup was different, the parents all standing too close to the curb. Karen explains that some people got hurt at a school far away but that Drew's school is safe. Drew asks, 'How do you know?' Karen doesn't have a good answer. Lockdown drills begin the following fall. Crouch under your desk, be very quiet, wait for the all-clear.
developmental_impact: The unaskable question — 'How do you know it's safe?' — and the non-answer. Drew learns that adults don't always know. He files this away without knowing he's filing it.

event: Greg's division at Alcatel restructures. He isn't laid off but three men on his team are. He comes home one Thursday with a box of things from his desk — 'just cleaning out,' he says — and is irritable for two months. Karen picks up more real estate shifts. Nana Carol starts picking Drew up from school on Tuesdays and Thursdays. Greg and Karen argue about money for the first time Drew can hear. Not yelling — the Mitchells don't yell — but the kitchen gets very quiet after dinner and Drew learns to read the silence.
developmental_impact: First crack in the suburban promise. Economic anxiety enters the household. Drew learns to read emotional atmosphere — silence is data. Emotional inhibition schema reinforced: if nobody talks about it, you don't either.

event: September 11. Drew is in second grade, Mrs. Pollard's class. The principal comes on the intercom at 9:15. Mrs. Pollard turns off the TV before the second tower falls, but not before the first. Some kids get picked up early. Drew doesn't — Karen is showing a house in McKinney and doesn't hear about it until 10:30. She arrives at 11, mascara wrong, and holds him in the parking lot for a long time without saying anything. That night the whole family watches the news together. Even Megan is quiet. Greg says, 'This changes everything,' and Drew believes him because Greg doesn't say things like that.
developmental_impact: The defining generational memory. Drew is old enough to remember the images, the silence in the school hallway, his mother's face. The world is revealed as breakable. This is the event that ends the absorbent years — the first time Drew processes a shared reality with conscious awareness.

event: Post-9/11 shift. Drew makes a quiet internal decision: he will be useful. He begins helping around the house unprompted, reads voraciously at Nana Carol's, engages with school as skill-building rather than compliance. Builds a working pulley for a class project. The organizing principle crystallizes: competence as security.
developmental_impact: Agency threshold reached. Drew's first self-directed orientation: convert anxiety into productivity, understand how things work, lighten the load on the people he loves. The emotional inhibition schema is reinforced — he processes the world through doing, not through feeling.
